# DSP Architecture: Nebula

**CRITICAL CONTRACT:** This specification is immutable during Stages 1-4 implementation. Stage 1 Planning cannot proceed without this file. Stage 3 (DSP) implements this exact architecture.

**Generated by:** Stage 0 Research
**Date:** 2026-04-24
**Referenced by:** Stage 1 (Planning), Stage 3 (DSP Implementation)
**Purpose:** DSP specification defining processing components, signal flow, and JUCE module usage

---

## Core Components

### Reverb Engine

- **JUCE Class:** `juce::dsp::Reverb`
- **Purpose:** Generate lo-fi algorithmic reverb with controllable room size and decay density. The Freeverb-style Schroeder algorithm (8 parallel comb filters + 4 series allpass filters) produces the natural lo-fi character called for in the brief — dense, slightly diffuse, not studio-clean.
- **Parameters Affected:** `size`
- **Configuration:**
  - `size` (0.0–1.0) maps directly to `reverbParams.roomSize` (0.0–1.0)
  - `damping` fixed at 0.4 (moderately dark default — enhances lo-fi feel; high frequencies already attenuated by the Mistero filter)
  - `wetLevel` set to 1.0 — DryWetMixer handles the dry/wet blend externally
  - `dryLevel` set to 0.0 — no dry signal passes through the reverb unit itself
  - `width` fixed at 1.0 (full stereo spread)
  - `freezeMode` set to 0.0 (disabled)
  - Size also influences perceived tail length: larger room = longer, denser tail

### Mistero Lowpass Filter

- **JUCE Class:** `juce::dsp::ProcessorDuplicator<juce::dsp::IIR::Filter<float>, juce::dsp::IIR::Coefficients<float>>`
- **Purpose:** Apply a 2nd-order Butterworth lowpass filter **exclusively to the wet (reverb) path**. The Mistero knob progressively darkens the reverb tail, simulating high-frequency absorption (heavy air, tape head gap losses, room materials). The dry signal is never affected.
- **Parameters Affected:** `mistero`
- **Configuration:**
  - Cutoff frequency: `mistero` parameter maps directly (200 Hz – 8000 Hz), exponential scale
  - Q factor: 0.707 (Butterworth — maximally flat, no ringing, musical rolloff)
  - Applied after the reverb engine, before the DryWetMixer blend step
  - Coefficients recalculated each `processBlock()` call when `mistero` changes
  - `juce::dsp::IIR::Coefficients<float>::makeLowPass(sampleRate, cutoffHz, 0.707f)`
  - Module: `juce_dsp`

### Noise Generator (Hiss)

- **JUCE Class:** Custom implementation — no dedicated JUCE noise generator class
- **Purpose:** Generate low-level analog tape hiss that is blended into the wet path only. The noise gives the reverb tail an organic, lived-in quality, evoking old recordings and tape machines. The noise level is fixed (not user-controlled) and kept subtle (~-40 to -45 dBFS relative to unit gain input).
- **Parameters Affected:** None (internal constant — not user-facing)
- **Configuration:**
  - Algorithm: Voss–McCartney pink noise approximation (6-stage octave-band summing of white noise sequences)
  - Pink noise preferred over white noise: perceptually flatter, sounds more like tape hiss (white sounds harsh/electronic)
  - `juce::Random` used as the PRNG (`juce::Random::getSystemRandom()`)
  - Noise amplitude: approximately 0.003–0.006 linear gain (~-44 to -40 dBFS) — audible but subtle
  - Mixed additively into the wet signal buffer after reverb + Mistero filter, before DryWetMixer blend
  - Noise amplitude scales with the `mix` parameter proportionally — when mix is 0, noise contribution is zero

### Dry/Wet Mixer

- **JUCE Class:** `juce::dsp::DryWetMixer<float>`
- **Purpose:** Linear blend between the unprocessed dry signal and the fully-processed wet path (reverb + Mistero filter + hiss). The `mix` parameter at 0% delivers 100% dry; at 100% delivers 100% wet. Default 40% wet matches the brief.
- **Parameters Affected:** `mix`
- **Configuration:**
  - `setMixingRule(juce::dsp::DryWetMixingRule::linear)` — linear crossfade per creative brief requirement ("bilanciamento lineare")
  - `setWetMixProportion(mix)` where `mix` is the normalized 0.0–1.0 float from the `mix` parameter
  - `pushDrySamples()` called before reverb processing (captures clean input)
  - `mixWetSamples()` called after noise injection (completes the blend)
  - Module: `juce_dsp`

---

## Processing Chain

```
Input (stereo)
  |
  +---> DryWetMixer.pushDrySamples()   [captures unprocessed dry]
  |
  v
Reverb Engine (juce::dsp::Reverb)      <- size parameter
  |
  v
Mistero Lowpass Filter (IIR::Filter)   <- mistero parameter (Hz)
  |
  v
Noise Injection (custom pink noise)    [additive mix into wet buffer]
  |
  v
DryWetMixer.mixWetSamples()            <- mix parameter (0.0–1.0)
  |
  v
Output (stereo)
```

**Routing notes:**
- The dry signal path is 100% bypassed through the DryWetMixer — it never touches the reverb, filter, or noise
- The wet path carries: reverb output -> Mistero filter -> +noise
- Noise is injected additively AFTER the Mistero filter so it is also attenuated by the filter (maintaining tonal coherence — noise should feel "inside" the dark reverb space, not on top of it)
- Alternative: noise before Mistero filter. Both are valid; post-filter chosen for tonal consistency
- No feedback loops beyond the internal comb filter feedback inside `juce::dsp::Reverb`
- No sidechain inputs

---

## System Architecture

### State Persistence

**What state is saved:**
- APVTS parameters: `mistero`, `size`, `mix` — all three saved automatically via `AudioProcessorValueTreeState`
- No additional custom state (no file paths, no lock buttons, no non-parameter state)

**Serialization format:**
- APVTS XML via ValueTree (automatic)
- `getStateInformation()` / `setStateInformation()` follow standard pattern (see DriveVerb reference)

**JUCE classes:**
- `juce::AudioProcessorValueTreeState` — parameter persistence (automatic)

**Restore behavior:**
- All parameters have defaults (mistero=6000Hz, size=0.5, mix=0.4)
- Corrupted XML: fall back to defaults silently

---

## Parameter Mapping

| Parameter ID | Type  | Range          | Default | DSP Component         | Usage                                                     |
|-------------|-------|----------------|---------|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| `mistero`   | Float | 200 – 8000 Hz  | 6000 Hz | Mistero Lowpass Filter | Cutoff frequency — lower = darker/more suffocated reverb tail |
| `size`      | Float | 0.0 – 1.0      | 0.5     | Reverb Engine         | Maps to `reverbParams.roomSize` (0.0–1.0)                |
| `mix`       | Float | 0.0 – 1.0      | 0.4     | Dry/Wet Mixer         | Wet proportion in linear blend                            |

**Parameter skew notes:**
- `mistero`: Use exponential/logarithmic NormalisableRange skew (~0.3) so the knob feels perceptually linear in pitch space. At default 6000 Hz, small left turns produce dramatic darkening; this is musically appropriate.
- `size`: Linear (0.0–1.0, normalized). Direct mapping to `roomSize`.
- `mix`: Linear (0.0–1.0). Straightforward wet/dry proportion.

---

## Algorithm Details

### Reverb Engine (juce::dsp::Reverb)

**Algorithm:** Freeverb-style Schroeder–Moorer reverberator (JUCE built-in)
- 8 parallel feedback comb filters with a 1st-order lowpass filter in each feedback path
- 4 series Schroeder allpass filters for diffusion
- Stereo output via decorrelated left/right delay tuning

**Implementation notes:**
- Use `juce::dsp::ProcessSpec` in `prepareToPlay()` — NOT `setSampleRate()` (critical patterns #17)
- Use `reverb.prepare(spec)` and `reverb.process(context)` — NOT `processMono()` / `processStereo()`
- `reverbParams.roomSize` = `size` value (already 0.0–1.0, no conversion needed)
- `reverbParams.damping` = 0.4 fixed. This makes the reverb inherently dark without Mistero being fully closed — amplifies lo-fi character
- `reverbParams.wetLevel` = 1.0, `reverbParams.dryLevel` = 0.0 (DryWetMixer handles the mix externally)
- Call `reverb.setParameters(reverbParams)` every processBlock — cheap operation, no latency

### Mistero Lowpass Filter

**Algorithm:** 2nd-order Butterworth IIR lowpass (biquad)
- Transfer function: H(z) = b0 + b1*z^-1 + b2*z^-2 / (1 + a1*z^-1 + a2*z^-2)
- Butterworth: maximally flat passband, -3dB at cutoff, 12dB/oct rolloff

**Implementation notes:**
- Use `juce::dsp::ProcessorDuplicator` to apply same coefficients to both stereo channels
- Update coefficients in `processBlock()`:
  ```
  *filterProcessor.state = *juce::dsp::IIR::Coefficients<float>::makeLowPass(
      getSampleRate(), misteroHz, 0.707f
  );
  ```
- `misteroHz` comes from `parameters.getRawParameterValue("mistero")->load()` (atomic read, real-time safe)
- Exponential mapping for `mistero` knob to achieve perceptual linearity in the audio range:
  - At 8000 Hz: minimal effect, reverb sounds relatively open
  - At 1000 Hz: heavy darkening, romantic/mysterious quality
  - At 200 Hz: nearly subterranean, full suffocation — extreme darkness
- No bypass zone needed (any value 200–8000 Hz is valid; 8000 Hz already close to open)
- Coefficient update each block: acceptable CPU cost for a single biquad

### Noise Generator (Pink Hiss)

**Algorithm:** Voss–McCartney octave-band pink noise approximation (6 stages)
- 6 independent white noise sequences, each updated at half the rate of the previous
- Sum all 6 sequences per sample, normalized to unit amplitude
- Result approximates 1/f power spectral density (pink noise) with -3dB/oct roll-off

**Implementation notes:**
- `juce::Random` instance for each of 6 bands (or share one, advance state appropriately)
- Per-sample generation in processBlock() loop — lightweight (bit operations + float multiply)
- Noise amplitude constant: `kNoiseGain = 0.004f` (~-48 dBFS, subtle presence)
- Noise is added to each sample of both channels independently
- Scale by `mix` parameter so when mix=0 there is zero noise contribution (silence stays silent)
- No separate noise envelope following the reverb tail — JUCE's reverb already attenuates noise naturally through the dry/wet blend (when mix=0, no noise; at mix=1, full noise, but reverb tail is loud enough to mask it)
- Simpler alternative considered: white noise with a fixed one-pole lowpass filter. Pink noise chosen for more tape-like character.

### Dry/Wet Mixer

**Algorithm:** Linear interpolation crossfade
- `output = (1 - mix) * dry + mix * wet`
- `juce::dsp::DryWetMixingRule::linear` implements this directly

**Implementation notes:**
- `pushDrySamples(block)` before any wet-path processing
- `mixWetSamples(block)` after all wet processing (reverb + filter + noise)
- `setWetMixProportion(mix)` where mix = `parameters.getRawParameterValue("mix")->load()`
- DryWetMixer handles latency compensation internally (important for host automation consistency)

---

## Integration Points

### Feature Dependencies

- Reverb Engine is the first stage in the wet path; Mistero Filter depends on its output
- Mistero Filter output feeds into the noise injection stage
- Noise injection completes the wet signal; DryWetMixer depends on this being complete
- DryWetMixer depends on dry capture (pushDrySamples) happening before wet processing begins

### Parameter Interactions

- `size` affects reverb density and perceived tail length. Larger size → longer tail → noise rides longer in the mix. This is intentional: more "space" means more analog hiss presence.
- `mix` controls both wet signal level AND effective noise level simultaneously (noise is part of the wet path). At low mix values, both reverb and hiss are attenuated equally — the dry signal dominates.
- `mistero` and `size` interact perceptually: with a large room and fully closed Mistero (200 Hz cutoff), the result is very dark and immersive. A small room with Mistero open (8 kHz) sounds more like a small dark chamber. Both are musically useful.
- `mistero` does NOT affect the dry signal. A guitar chord stays bright even when the reverb tail is completely smothered.

### Processing Order Requirements

1. **Capture dry signal** (`DryWetMixer.pushDrySamples(block)`) — MUST happen first, before any processing modifies the buffer
2. **Apply reverb** (`reverb.process(context)`) — transforms dry input into wet reverb signal in-place
3. **Apply Mistero filter** (`filterProcessor.process(context)`) — darkens the wet reverb signal in-place
4. **Inject noise** (per-sample additive loop) — adds hiss to the already-filtered wet signal
5. **Blend dry and wet** (`DryWetMixer.mixWetSamples(block)`) — MUST happen last, after all wet processing

Why order matters:
- Step 1 before steps 2–4: if dry capture happens after reverb, the dry signal is already wet
- Step 3 before step 4: noise is injected into the already-darkened signal for tonal coherence
- Step 5 after all wet processing: the mixer needs the complete wet signal to blend correctly

### Thread Boundaries

**Audio thread (processBlock):**
- All DSP processing (reverb, filter, noise, dry/wet mix)
- Parameter reads via `parameters.getRawParameterValue("x")->load()` (atomic, real-time safe)
- Noise generation (PRNG state is local, no cross-thread access)

**Message thread:**
- APVTS parameter writes from WebView UI (via relay system)
- Plugin state save/load
- UI repaints

**No background thread needed:**
- No file I/O
- No long-running operations
- All processing is real-time compatible

**Communication:**
- APVTS parameters: atomic reads (audio thread) / atomic writes via APVTS (message thread)
- No custom synchronization needed

---

## Implementation Risks

### Reverb Engine

**Complexity:** LOW
**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- `juce::dsp::Reverb` is a well-known JUCE class with documented patterns
- The only risk is the API mismatch (old vs. new API) — already documented in critical patterns #17

**Alternative approaches:**
1. Custom Freeverb C++ implementation — lower risk of API confusion, higher implementation time
2. `juce::Reverb` (old class) — lower quality API, deprecated style

**Fallback architecture:**
- Primary: `juce::dsp::Reverb` (modern DSP pipeline)
- Fallback: Custom 4-comb + 2-allpass Schroeder reverb if JUCE reverb quality is unsatisfactory

**Mitigation:**
- Follow critical pattern #17 exactly: `prepare(spec)` + `process(context)` only

---

### Mistero Lowpass Filter

**Complexity:** LOW–MEDIUM
**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- Coefficient update on every processBlock() is safe for a slowly-varying parameter (no per-sample modulation)
- Potential for audio glitches if coefficients update too aggressively (user sweeping the knob rapidly)
- `juce::dsp::IIR::Filter` does not have built-in smoothing — rapid knob movement could cause coefficient discontinuities

**Alternative approaches:**
1. `juce::dsp::StateVariableTPTFilter` — designed for real-time parameter changes, no artifact risk. Slightly different filter character (same Butterworth shape, but internally smoothed). RECOMMENDED if artifacts are observed.
2. Smooth the `mistero` parameter value using `juce::SmoothedValue<float>` before updating filter coefficients — prevents discontinuities at any knob speed.

**Fallback architecture:**
- Primary: `juce::dsp::ProcessorDuplicator<juce::dsp::IIR::Filter>` with per-block coefficient update
- Fallback: `juce::dsp::StateVariableTPTFilter` with continuous cutoff update (same module, different class)

**Mitigation:**
- Add `juce::SmoothedValue<float>` for misteroHz with a 20ms smoothing time. This prevents zipper noise at all realistic knob speeds and is a lightweight addition.

---

### Noise Generator

**Complexity:** LOW
**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- Custom implementation (no JUCE class) — must be written from scratch
- Pink noise approximation (Voss–McCartney) is simple but can sound noisy/colored if not tuned
- Noise amplitude must be carefully calibrated to avoid being too loud or inaudible

**Alternative approaches:**
1. White noise with single-pole lowpass filter (~100 Hz cutoff) — simpler than Voss–McCartney, still tape-like
2. White noise only — harsher, less tape-like, but trivially simple
3. Pre-recorded tape hiss sample looped — highest quality, but adds file I/O complexity (rejected: adds Tier 5 complexity for marginal gain)

**Fallback architecture:**
- Primary: Voss–McCartney 6-stage pink noise
- Fallback 1: White noise filtered with one-pole lowpass (`y[n] = 0.99*y[n-1] + 0.01*x[n]`)
- Fallback 2: Pure white noise at very low amplitude

**Mitigation:**
- Start with Fallback 1 (simpler, tune amplitude by ear), upgrade to pink noise if needed
- Amplitude constant `kNoiseGain = 0.004f` can be tuned during validation

---

### Dry/Wet Mixer

**Complexity:** LOW
**Risk Level:** LOW

**Risk factors:**
- `juce::dsp::DryWetMixer` is a well-supported class
- Linear mixing rule is simpler and matches brief ("bilanciamento lineare")
- No known risks

**Fallback architecture:**
- If `juce::dsp::DryWetMixer` causes issues: manual linear blend in processBlock() loop
  ```cpp
  output[i] = (1.0f - mix) * dry[i] + mix * wet[i];
  ```

---

### Overall Project Risk

**Overall complexity:** LOW
- All features use well-supported JUCE classes or simple custom code
- No FFT, no modulation LFO, no file I/O, no MIDI, no feedback loops
- 3 parameters, all float, all straightforward mappings

**Highest risk component:** Mistero Lowpass Filter coefficient smoothing
- Minor risk: zipper noise on rapid knob movement
- Mitigation: `juce::SmoothedValue<float>` on misteroHz

**Recommended approach:**
1. Implement DryWetMixer + Reverb first (core, low risk)
2. Add Mistero filter with SmoothedValue (medium, well-documented)
3. Add noise generator last (tune amplitude by ear)
4. Validate all three together

---

## Architecture Decisions

### Decision: juce::dsp::Reverb over Custom Freeverb

**Decision:** Use `juce::dsp::Reverb` (JUCE built-in, Freeverb-based) rather than a custom Schroeder implementation.

**Rationale:**
- `juce::dsp::Reverb` IS the Freeverb algorithm (8 comb + 4 allpass) — exactly what the brief calls for
- Mature, tested, no bug risk in the reverb core
- Integrates natively with `juce::dsp::ProcessSpec` and `AudioBlock` pipeline
- DriveVerb and FlutterVerb already prove this class works for this plugin type

**Alternatives considered:**
1. Custom Freeverb port: Higher implementation time, same result, higher bug risk in filter tunings
2. Third-party reverb library: Adds external dependency, licensing concerns

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- Less control over internal comb/allpass tuning (but built-in damping parameter covers the lo-fi character needed)

---

### Decision: Fixed Noise Amplitude (Not User-Facing)

**Decision:** Noise/hiss level is not a user-facing parameter. It is a fixed internal constant.

**Rationale:**
- Creative brief specifies exactly 3 knobs: Mistero, Size, Mix. Adding a "Hiss" knob would violate the design.
- The lo-fi character should be inherent and non-negotiable — it's part of the sonic identity
- Noise at -44 dBFS is audible when reverb tail is present but never intrusive

**Alternatives considered:**
1. Hiss knob (0–100%): More flexible, but dilutes the "opinionated" plugin identity
2. No noise at all: Removes a key differentiating character element mentioned explicitly in the brief

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- Some users may want more or less hiss — this is not possible (intentional design choice)

---

### Decision: Noise Injected After Mistero Filter

**Decision:** Inject pink noise into the wet path after the Mistero filter (not before).

**Rationale:**
- When Mistero is fully closed (200 Hz cutoff), the reverb tail is essentially a low-frequency drone
- Adding noise post-filter means the noise is also filtered by Mistero, so at 200 Hz the hiss becomes a very low rumble — more atmospheric, more coherent with the reverb character
- If noise were added before the filter, at 200 Hz the hiss would still be bright (full spectrum) while the reverb is very dark — tonal mismatch

**Alternatives considered:**
1. Noise before Mistero filter: Simpler code, but tonally incoherent at extreme Mistero settings
2. Noise in parallel (unfiltered): Could work if distinct hiss texture is desired regardless of Mistero

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- At low Mistero values, hiss becomes a low-frequency rumble rather than high-frequency hiss — this is considered atmospheric/desirable for the plugin concept

---

### Decision: Linear Dry/Wet Mixing Rule

**Decision:** Use `juce::dsp::DryWetMixingRule::linear` rather than `::balanced` (equal-power).

**Rationale:**
- Creative brief explicitly states "bilanciamento lineare" (linear balance)
- Linear mixing preserves the full dry signal amplitude when mix < 0.5, which is appropriate for a subtle reverb effect
- Equal-power would boost the combined signal at 50% mix, potentially introducing perceived loudness changes

**Alternatives considered:**
1. `DryWetMixingRule::balanced` (equal-power): More common in professional plugins, prevents perceived volume drop at 50%
2. Manual linear blend: Same result, more code

**Tradeoffs accepted:**
- Linear mixing causes a small perceived volume change around 50% mix (normal for linear crossfade)

---

## Special Considerations

### Thread Safety

- All three parameter reads use `parameters.getRawParameterValue("x")->load()` (atomic float, real-time safe)
- Noise PRNG state (`juce::Random`) is accessed only on the audio thread — no cross-thread contention
- Filter coefficients updated per-block on audio thread only — no concurrency issues
- `juce::SmoothedValue<float>` for misteroHz operates on audio thread only (no sharing)

### Performance

- Reverb (`juce::dsp::Reverb`): ~5–15% single core at 48kHz stereo — the dominant DSP cost
- Mistero filter (single biquad per channel): ~1% CPU — negligible
- Pink noise generation (6-stage Voss–McCartney): ~1% CPU — negligible
- Dry/wet mixer: ~1% CPU — negligible
- **Total estimated:** ~10–20% single core at 48kHz stereo — well within acceptable limits

### Denormal Protection

- Use `juce::ScopedNoDenormals noDenormals;` at the top of `processBlock()` — standard pattern
- `juce::dsp::Reverb` handles denormals internally in its comb/allpass feedback loops
- Pink noise generator always produces non-zero values, preventing denormals in the wet path

### Sample Rate Handling

- `prepareToPlay()` creates `juce::dsp::ProcessSpec` with sampleRate, maximumBlockSize, numChannels
- All DSP components re-initialized in `prepareToPlay()` via `component.prepare(spec)`
- Mistero filter coefficients reference `getSampleRate()` — recalculated correctly after sample rate change
- SmoothedValue for misteroHz: reset in `prepareToPlay()` with `smoother.reset(sampleRate, 0.02)` (20ms ramp)
- Pink noise generator: sample-rate independent (amplitude calibration only)

### Latency

- `juce::dsp::Reverb`: zero reported latency (internal latency absorbed into reverb effect)
- Total plugin latency: 0 samples reported via `getLatencySamples()` — no compensation needed
- Note: internal reverb processing starts immediately (first samples are reverb output, not silence)

---

## Research References

### Professional Plugins Researched

1. **Valhalla VintageVerb**
   - Reference for "dark vintage" reverb character
   - The 1970s color mode reduces bandwidth to ~10kHz max and is "dark and noisy with random artifacts"
   - Informs our choice of fixed damping=0.4 and the noise/hiss approach
   - Key observation: lo-fi reverb character comes from bandwidth limiting + noise, not from poor algorithms

2. **Meris Mercury7**
   - Hardware algorithmic reverb inspired by Yamaha CS-80 / Blade Runner aesthetic
   - Has dedicated "Lo Frequency" damping knob — analogous to Nebula's Mistero concept
   - Proves the "darkening filter on wet path only" approach is a professional pattern
   - Confirms two-algorithm approach: plate (fast buildup) and cathedral (slow buildup)

3. **OTO Biscuit**
   - Lo-fi bitcrusher/effect unit referenced in the brief
   - Teaches that lo-fi character comes from deliberate degradation (noise, bandwidth limiting)
   - Supports the decision to add analog hiss as a fixed character element

4. **Denise Bad Tape**
   - Has explicit HISS parameter (0–100%) — proves hiss is a distinct controllable effect
   - Reinforces that noise is mixed additively into the processed signal path (not the dry path)
   - Amplitude reference: subtle tape hiss is at approximately -40 to -48 dBFS

5. **Freeverb (open source)**
   - Schroeder-Moorer architecture: 8 comb filters (delay times ~25–45ms, incommensurate) + 4 allpass
   - The natural lo-fi character comes from the comb filter resonances creating coloration — intentional
   - `juce::dsp::Reverb` is a JUCE implementation of this algorithm

### JUCE Documentation

- **`juce::dsp::Reverb`**: Built-in Freeverb implementation. Parameters: `roomSize`, `damping`, `wetLevel`, `dryLevel`, `width`, `freezeMode`. API: `prepare(spec)` + `setParameters(params)` + `process(context)`. Module: `juce_dsp`. Pattern: Critical pattern #17.
- **`juce::dsp::DryWetMixer<float>`**: Latency-compensated dry/wet blending. API: `prepare(spec)` + `pushDrySamples(block)` + `setWetMixProportion(x)` + `mixWetSamples(block)`. Module: `juce_dsp`.
- **`juce::dsp::ProcessorDuplicator<juce::dsp::IIR::Filter<float>, juce::dsp::IIR::Coefficients<float>>`**: Applies stereo IIR filter from mono coefficients. API: `prepare(spec)` + `process(context)` + coefficient update via `*filterProcessor.state = *Coefficients::makeLowPass(...)`. Module: `juce_dsp`.
- **`juce::dsp::IIR::Coefficients<float>::makeLowPass(sampleRate, frequency, Q)`**: Factory for Butterworth biquad lowpass. Returns `ReferenceCountedObjectPtr<Coefficients>`. Module: `juce_dsp`.
- **`juce::Random`**: PRNG for noise generation. `nextFloat()` returns [0, 1). For noise: `(random.nextFloat() * 2.0f - 1.0f)`. Thread-safe instance per audio thread.
- **`juce::SmoothedValue<float>`**: Per-block parameter smoothing. `reset(sampleRate, rampSeconds)` + `setTargetValue(x)` + `getNextValue()` per sample. Module: `juce_core` (always available).

### Technical Resources

- Freeverb algorithm — CCRMA Stanford (Julius O. Smith III, Physical Audio Signal Processing): https://ccrma.stanford.edu/~jos/pasp/Freeverb.html
- Voss-McCartney pink noise algorithm — MusicDSP.org: https://www.musicdsp.org/en/latest/Synthesis/220-trammell-pink-noise-c-class.html
- Schroeder Reverbs history — Valhalla DSP blog: https://valhalladsp.com/2009/05/30/schroeder-reverbs-the-forgotten-algorithm/
- JUCE IIR filter forum discussion on real-time coefficient updates: https://forum.juce.com/t/iir-filter-is-per-sample-modulation-possible/27598

---

## Notes

- `juce::dsp::Reverb` uses the Freeverb algorithm internally — this exactly matches the creative brief's recommendation of "Schroeder o Freeverb (comb + allpass filters)". No custom reverb needed.
- The `damping` parameter of `juce::dsp::Reverb` provides a gentle pre-filtering inside the comb filter feedback loops. Set to 0.4 (moderately dark) to complement the Mistero external filter.
- The noise amplitude constant `kNoiseGain = 0.004f` is a starting point; tune during validation.
- No UI visualization (VU meters, waveform displays) called for in the brief. GUI is three knobs only — this keeps Stage 3 (GUI) simple.
- Bus configuration: stereo in / stereo out, standard effect (not synth). `BusesProperties().withInput("Input", AudioChannelSet::stereo(), true).withOutput("Output", AudioChannelSet::stereo(), true)` in constructor. Critical pattern #4.
- `IS_SYNTH` flag: FALSE. `NEEDS_MIDI_INPUT`: FALSE. Standard effect.
- No NEEDS_WEB_BROWSER in CMakeLists unless WebView UI is used (TBD at Stage 3 GUI).
